story:
Enzo dreams of home. Sunlit grass. A picnic table crowded with tossed salad, steaming corn, grilled cheeseburgers on split poppy-seed buns slathered with pesto mayonnaise, tomatoes and onions slick with juice. Brio Limonata and Peroni. Lemonade for the kids. Laughter layered over the hiss of a sprinkler's spinning crown. Faces he knows but cannot name. And a voice only he can hear: Why didn't you save them?

He wakes to sterile white, a mylar blanket like thin metal over his chest, Velcro biting his hips, the pod door opening with a wet squelch of vacuum. Recycled air, chlorine-clean, tinted with his own sour sweat. He knows his name—Enzo—and nothing else. A sun-baked can, then eclipse, then sun again, every ninety minutes, turning him into a reheated meal. Where am I?

The memory comes: the Red Death. Streets of Bologna red on red—tiles and blood—air heavy with rot and garlic sweetness. Masks and gloves, as if walking outside were a spacewalk. Nature not pruning but ripping, an enraged gardener tearing up the bed. Shame pricks his mind. What have I done?

Obadiah speaks through the PA with courtly calm. "Good morning, Master Enzo. We remain in orbit above Earth." Enzo's heart thuds, speeds. Fever. A blood draw. "You are suffering dissociative retrograde amnesia," Obadiah says. "Usually trauma-induced. Your 'scrape with death' is classified." Classified by whom? "Dr. Zipkin classified the mission parameters before your launch, sir."

The sickness hits hard. Cramps. Trips to the head until he's dehydrated and floating, eyes full of glittering orbs that don't blink away. A vision of Earth from the cupola—flame sweeping the globe—then a drowning veil of red. He seals himself in the pod, hyperventilating against fogged glass, sedates himself, falls into the same two dreams: the backyard, and a lab where a man in a biohazard suit answers to Ahab.

Red dots bloom on his skin, sandy to the touch. Capillaries gone wrong. "You're positive for the Red Death," Obadiah confirms. "No cure yet." Enzo argues virology: viruses that kill their hosts fail, unless given time to grow milder. Time Earth doesn't have. He tries to reach the world; there's no world to reach. "Dr. Zipkin restricted all outside communications and sealed your access to mission files," Obadiah says. Why would he do that?

Rage flares. Panels tear, sparks bloom and drift. He stops, panting, realizing that destroying the ship kills Obadiah too. The hemorrhages spread: forearms, neck, chest, his breath ragged, blood freckles the pod's visor. Hallucinations: a ship pacing his, an astronaut's wave melting into a clown's painted thumbs-down. Then the lab returns, too sharp to be only a dream.

Ahab Zipkin, Herod Mederi Laboratory, Tuscany's hundred-year cypresses outside. Ahab the tin man, heart locked in a vault. "If we sacrifice all our young to secure the future, so be it." Enzo discovers a potential vaccine to isolate the virus; Ahab intends to sell it to the highest bidders instead of saving the many. Alarms blare. Guards drag Enzo out. Ahab uploads schematics to Obadiah: test it on one hundred infected. A beating, a tumble down a hill, a cyborg's hands, a pod fired into the dark.

Enzo wakes slick with sweat and blood, pink as a newborn. The rash retreats. "How many of the hundred?" "You are the only survivor, Master Enzo." They descend to the lazaret at Livorno, a rock watching the harbor mouth, to wait out quarantine. The grid is dead. No lights from space. At least ninety percent of humanity is gone. Survivors, if any, are islands.

Why me? "Because Ahab's virus was drawn from your blood. You were its source." The memory breaks open. Enzo weeps. He cannot undo what was done. But he can atone. Rest now. Then the antidote.
